meta.title: Elixir & Phoenix
meta.description: Elixir applications with OTP, GenServer, Phoenix LiveView, and Ecto

items.text: Is pattern matching used effectively instead of conditional logic?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Elixir's pattern matching should be used in function heads, case, and with instead of nested if/cond when matching on structure.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Pattern matching is clearer, more idiomatic, and lets the compiler help catch missing cases. Nested conditionals obscure intent.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for nested if/cond that could be replaced by multiple function clauses or case with pattern matching.
baseContent.exampleComment: This nested if checking map keys could be a multi-clause function with pattern matching: def handle(%{type: :order} = event), etc.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use pattern matching in function heads for dispatch. Reserve if/cond for boolean conditions.

items.text: Is the pipe operator used for clear data transformation chains?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The pipe operator |> should be used for sequential transformations. Don't pipe into functions where the data isn't the first argument.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Well-used pipes make data flow obvious. Misused pipes (single-step, wrong argument position) make code harder to read.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that pipe chains flow naturally. Verify piped data is the first argument. Watch for single-step pipes (unnecessary).
baseContent.exampleComment: This pipes into Enum.map with an anonymous function that ignores the piped value. The pipe isn't adding clarity here.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use pipes for multi-step transformations. Don't pipe for a single function call.

items.text: Is the 'with' statement used correctly for chaining operations that may fail?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use 'with' for chaining operations that return {:ok, value} or {:error, reason}. Handle the else clause for failures.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Missing else clauses in 'with' cause cryptic MatchError crashes. Overly long 'with' chains become hard to debug.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that 'with' statements have else clauses handling error cases. Verify they're not too long (3-5 clauses max).
baseContent.exampleComment: This 'with' has no else clause. If any step returns {:error, reason}, you'll get a MatchError instead of a clear error.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always include else in 'with' statements. Keep chains to 3-5 steps.

items.text: Does the code follow 'let it crash' philosophy appropriately?

baseContent.whatItMeans: In Elixir/OTP, unexpected errors should crash the process and let the supervisor restart it. Don't catch errors defensively for everything.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Over-defensive error handling hides bugs and prevents supervisors from doing their job. Under-handling expected errors crashes user-facing processes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that expected errors (user input, API responses) are handled. Verify unexpected errors aren't silently rescued.
baseContent.exampleComment: This catch-all try/rescue hides real bugs. Let unexpected errors crash and handle only expected {:error, _} tuples.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Handle expected errors explicitly. Let unexpected errors crash so supervisors can restart cleanly.

items.text: Are typespecs defined for public functions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Public functions should have @spec annotations. Custom types should use @type or @typedoc for documentation.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Typespecs enable Dialyzer static analysis, serve as documentation, and help editors provide better autocomplete.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that public functions (def, not defp) have @spec. Verify specs match the actual return types.
baseContent.exampleComment: This public function lacks a @spec. Add @spec create_user(map()) :: {:ok, User.t()} | {:error, Ecto.Changeset.t()}.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Add @spec to public functions. Run Dialyzer in CI to catch type errors.

items.text: Is GenServer state kept minimal and its API well-defined?

baseContent.whatItMeans: GenServers should have a clear public API (client functions) wrapping call/cast. State should be minimal — don't use GenServer as a database.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Bloated GenServer state is lost on crash. Complex state makes debugging hard. Missing client API forces callers to know implementation details.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that GenServer has public functions wrapping call/cast. Verify state is minimal and can be reconstructed from persistent storage.
baseContent.exampleComment: This GenServer stores the full user list in state. If it crashes, all data is lost. Use ETS or a database for large datasets.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep GenServer state minimal. Provide a clean client API. Persist important data externally.

items.text: Is the supervision tree structured correctly?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Supervisors should group related processes. Use :one_for_one, :one_for_all, or :rest_for_one based on process dependencies.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Wrong supervision strategy causes cascading restarts or leaves dependent processes in broken states.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that the supervision strategy matches process dependencies. Verify child_spec options (restart, max_restarts) are appropriate.
baseContent.exampleComment: These processes depend on each other but use :one_for_one. Use :one_for_all or :rest_for_one so dependents restart together.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Choose supervision strategy based on process dependencies. Group related processes under sub-supervisors.

items.text: Are GenServers not creating bottlenecks (single process handling all requests)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: A single GenServer handling all requests serializes work. Use pooling (poolboy), Registry, or Task for concurrent work.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A single GenServer handling 1000 concurrent requests processes them one at a time, becoming the system bottleneck.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if a GenServer handles requests from many clients. Consider if the work could be parallelized with Task.async or a pool.
baseContent.exampleComment: This single GenServer handles all API calls sequentially. Use Task.Supervisor with async_nolink for concurrent processing.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Don't funnel concurrent work through a single GenServer. Use Task, pools, or per-entity processes.

items.text: Are process mailboxes protected from unbounded growth?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Processes that receive messages faster than they handle them will accumulate a backlog, eventually consuming all memory.
baseContent.whyItMatters: An overflowing mailbox causes OOM crashes. This is especially common with GenServer casts and PubSub subscriptions.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for high-volume cast/send patterns without backpressure. Verify GenServers handle messages fast enough for their expected load.
baseContent.exampleComment: This GenServer receives a cast for every HTTP request but does expensive work in handle_cast. Add backpressure or use a Task pool.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Design for backpressure. Use call instead of cast when the sender can wait. Monitor mailbox sizes.

items.text: Are changesets used for all data validation?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use Ecto changesets with validate_required, validate_format, validate_length, etc. for all input validation before database operations.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Bypassing changesets skips validation, allowing invalid data into the database. Changeset errors provide clear user feedback.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that inserts and updates go through changesets. Verify changesets validate all user-facing fields.
baseContent.exampleComment: This inserts data with Repo.insert(%User{email: email}) bypassing the changeset. Use User.changeset(%User{}, %{email: email}).
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always use changesets for inserts and updates. Never bypass validation.

items.text: Are associations preloaded to avoid N+1 queries?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Accessing associations without preloading raises or returns nil. Use Repo.preload(), from() with preload:, or join + preload.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Accessing unpreloaded associations either raises UndefinedFunctionError or silently returns incomplete data.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for association access in templates and serialization. Verify the query preloads the needed associations.
baseContent.exampleComment: This template accesses post.comments but the query doesn't preload comments. Add |> Repo.preload(:comments).
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Preload associations in the query, not in templates. Use join + preload for filtered associations.

items.text: Are Ecto migrations safe for zero-downtime deploys?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Migrations run before new code deploys. Renaming columns, removing columns, or changing types can break the running application.
baseContent.whyItMatters: During deployment, old code runs against the new schema. Breaking changes cause errors for all requests until the new code deploys.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the migration would break the currently running code. Renaming, removing, or changing column types requires a multi-step approach.
baseContent.exampleComment: Renaming this column will break the running code. Use a multi-step migration: add new column, backfill, deploy code, remove old column.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Migrations must be backward-compatible with the currently running code.

items.text: Are multi-step operations wrapped in Repo.transaction or Multi?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use Ecto.Multi or Repo.transaction for operations that must succeed or fail atomically.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without transactions, a failure between two inserts leaves the database in an inconsistent state.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for sequences of Repo.insert/update that should be atomic. Verify Ecto.Multi is used for complex operations.
baseContent.exampleComment: Creating a user and their profile are separate Repo.insert calls. Use Ecto.Multi to make them atomic.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use Ecto.Multi for multi-step database operations. Handle rollbacks explicitly.

items.text: Are LiveView assigns minimal and properly managed?

baseContent.whatItMeans: LiveView assigns are sent to the client on every update. Keep them minimal. Use temporary_assigns for large lists.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Large assigns increase WebSocket payload size and memory usage per connection. Thousands of connections with bloated assigns can OOM.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check assign sizes in LiveView mount and handle_event. Look for large lists that should use temporary_assigns or streams.
baseContent.exampleComment: This LiveView assigns a list of 10K items. Use streams or temporary_assigns to avoid keeping all items in memory.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep LiveView assigns small. Use streams for large collections. Use temporary_assigns for append-only lists.

items.text: Are authentication and authorization enforced in the router pipeline?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use plugs in the router pipeline for authentication. Use on_mount hooks for LiveView authorization.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Missing auth plugs expose protected routes. LiveView must re-check auth on mount since WebSocket connections persist.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that protected route scopes have auth plugs. Verify LiveViews use on_mount for authorization checks.
baseContent.exampleComment: This LiveView has no on_mount auth check. A user who loses their session while connected will retain access.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Enforce auth in router pipelines for controllers and on_mount for LiveViews.

items.text: Is business logic in contexts, not in controllers or LiveViews?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Phoenix contexts (e.g., Accounts, Catalog) should encapsulate business logic. Controllers and LiveViews should only handle HTTP/WebSocket concerns.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Logic in controllers can't be reused in LiveViews and vice versa. Contexts provide a clean API that both can use.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for complex business logic in controllers or LiveView handle_event callbacks. It should be in context modules.
baseContent.exampleComment: This controller calculates order totals inline. Move this to the Orders context so it can be reused in the LiveView.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep controllers and LiveViews thin. Put business logic in contexts.

items.text: Is PubSub used correctly for real-time updates?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use Phoenix.PubSub for broadcasting updates to LiveViews. Subscribe in mount, broadcast from contexts, handle in handle_info.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Direct process messaging doesn't scale across nodes. PubSub works with distributed Erlang for multi-node deployments.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that subscriptions happen in mount. Verify broadcasts include only the data needed (not entire records).
baseContent.exampleComment: This broadcasts the entire user record on every update. Broadcast only the changed fields to reduce payload.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Subscribe in mount, broadcast from contexts. Keep broadcast payloads minimal.

items.text: Are context functions tested with meaningful assertions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Context modules should have thorough tests covering success, failure, and edge cases. Use ExUnit and Ecto sandbox.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Context functions contain business logic. Untested logic leads to regressions that are hard to catch in integration tests.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that context functions have test files. Verify tests cover valid input, invalid input, and edge cases.
baseContent.exampleComment: This new context function create_order/1 has no tests. Add tests for valid attrs, missing required fields, and invalid associations.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test every public context function. Cover success, validation failures, and edge cases.

items.text: Are tests using async: true where possible?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Tests that use the Ecto SQL sandbox can run concurrently with async: true. This significantly speeds up the test suite.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Synchronous tests run one at a time. A 500-test suite at 50ms each takes 25s sync but under 5s async.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for use MyApp.DataCase without async: true. Verify tests don't have shared state that prevents async execution.
baseContent.exampleComment: This test module uses async: false but doesn't share state with other tests. Set async: true for faster execution.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Default to async: true. Only use async: false for tests that require exclusive database access.

items.text: Are LiveView interactions tested with live/2 and render_* helpers?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Test LiveViews with conn |> live(), then use render_click, render_submit, render_change to simulate user interactions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: LiveView tests catch rendering bugs, event handling issues, and authorization problems that unit tests miss.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that LiveViews have corresponding test files using live/2. Verify tests simulate user interactions, not just initial render.
baseContent.exampleComment: This LiveView test only checks initial render. Add tests for form submission, event handling, and navigation.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test LiveView interactions, not just rendering. Use render_click/submit/change to simulate user actions.
